original_hypothesis.title: BlastGovernor.execute allows arbitrary external calls; mis-use could irreversibly reconfigure Blast yield governor mappings or drain held ETH
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: BlastGovernor exposes an owner-only `execute(to,value,data)` that can call arbitrary contracts with ETH. If misused (or owner key compromised), it can reconfigure Blast precompile settings for other contracts (via callBlastPrecompile/execute), redirecting yield or disabling claimability. This can also drain ETH held by BlastGovernor (receive() enabled).
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Operationally, this kind of universal executor is often required, but it increases blast radius. Validate whether BlastGovernor is intended as the sole governor for multiple contracts and whether safeguards (multisig policy, monitoring) exist outside the code.
deployment_assumptions: BlastGovernor is used as the configured Blast governor for multiple protocol contracts
Owner privileges are exercised operationally via execute/callBlastPrecompile
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: Blast.s.sol and MIMSwap.s.sol deploy BlastGovernor and pass blastGovernor into wrapped deployments (BlastCauldronV4 constructor; BlastMIMSwapRouter/Factory configureDefaultClaimables(governor)).

proof_of_signal.pattern: function execute(address to, uint256 value, bytes calldata data) external onlyOwner { (success, result) = to.call{value:value}(data); }

preconditions: BlastGovernor owner is malicious/compromised, or governance process allows unsafe calls
BlastGovernor holds ETH or is authorized as governor for yield claiming configurations

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Universal call primitive with value transfer; Blast precompile configuration is highly sensitive

operational_questions: Which contracts have BlastGovernor as configured governor in Blast precompile?
Is execute used in runbooks, and is it monitored/alerted?
What funds (ETH/yield) can accumulate on BlastGovernor address?
validation_requirements: Enumerate all contracts whose Blast governor mapping points to BlastGovernor
Simulate unsafe precompile config calls and assess reversibility

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Use execute() to call Blast precompile configuration methods for target contracts
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Redirect yield recipient/governor or disable claimability modes
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Optionally sweep ETH via execute to attacker-controlled address

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Control over yield configuration and potential yield redirection
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Yield claiming disabled or redirected; accounting assumptions broken
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium-High (depends on Blast precompile reversibility and governance access)
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Blast yield/points configuration across protocol contracts

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: execute() calls; unusual precompile calls; yield recipients changed
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Drop in expected yield receipts to feeTo

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
